caption.text: Throughout the last century, East Boston has been the home of numerous city housing, development, and neighborhood revitalization projects. Before the first public housing, Maverick Gardens, was developed in the early 1940s, East Boston was one of the city’s key industrial sites.
caption.title: Introduction

caption.text: In 1940, Boston [gained the funding](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) to begin work on a housing project in East Boston in Maverick Square. The project would be located between New St., Maverick St., Havre St., and Sumner St. Its primary aim would be to house the families of soldiers fighting in World War II, but would [also include](https://www.proquest.com/docview/839320215/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/6?sourcetype=Newspapers) some general low income housing..

The housing project looked very different from the homes there before. Previously, [as shown in this newspaper clip](https://www.proquest.com/docview/816891146/E2548D8D3BB647BEPQ/4?sourcetype=Newspapers), the buildings ranged in appearance and height, crafted seemingly of wood, some having porches and others having gabled roofs.

All of the buildings in the new project were three stories high, made of brick, and exactly uniform, spanning several blocks. These buildings stuck out in their surroundings, and some of the previous individuality and neighborhood charm was lost in new redevelopment.

How did East Bostonians earn a living? This map shows that industry on the waterfront was already prevalent here, serving as a shipyard for imports and the start of the railroad to ship the products across the state. By 1922, just 20 years before the public housing would begin construction, East Boston was already booming with industries like coal, wool, electricity, and storage.

East Boston residents had ample employment opportunites right on their doorstep. This photo depicts Sumner Street before the public housing development. On the right side, the many buildings lining the street housed families and working class Bostonians.
Just across the street sits Staples Coal Company...
...and just a block away at the intersection of New Street is Boston Cold Storage Company.
The neighborhood blended together with the factories and plants, meaning that these residents likely lived here for their proximity to work.
The industrial neighborhoods along the waterfront were also well-connected to the rest of Boston, thanks to the bustling trolley station in Maverick Square.

This lively image taken in 1918 shows Maverick Square as a gathering space, waiting for the trolley pictured in the center to arrive. It would have been convenient for residents here, who only had to step outside to reach their train station.
Maverick Square was also home to the Maverick House, a hotel built in 1835 renowned for its luxuriousness.

The Maverick House was not only a hotel marketed towards out-of-towners, but also a place for the community to come together and enjoy a game of billiards, cigars, or the luxury bar. College students used the hotel as a space to meet up and relax after classes. It was demolished in 1924 to make room for a new train station.

The East Boston housing project would primarily target families, meaning that the nearby area needed to provide adequate schooling for the children. Maverick Square's close proximity to schools may one of the reasons this particular location was chosen.
The closest school to the housing project was Fitton School, located on Havre Street and only a block away from some parts of the housing project

Fitton School was a Catholic school that [opened its doors to East Boston](https://www.proquest.com/docview/497371487/19F7D844D6574040PQ/1?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) in 1893. The school was named after Father Fitton, who was the pastor of the only Catholic Church in East Boston in 1855.

Located right next to the Catholic Church of the Holy Redeemer and the Notre Dame Convent, the Catholic church was a central aspect of the school’s operation and education. The school was quite small at its opening, [handing out only seven diplomas](https://www.proquest.com/docview/499341402/E1959FFF475C4D0CPQ/5?accountid=12826&sourcetype=Newspapers) in 1900.
The school remained open until 1974, when it became East Boston Central Catholic school…
…And still utilizes the original building today.
Residents of the East Boston public housing project had multiple kinds of churches to choose from. Besides the Catholic Church of the Holy Redeemer, nearby religious buildings included...
The Union Methodist Episcopal Church...
Maverick Congregational Church...
...and the Swedish Lutheran Church. This variety of churches shows that while East Boston was predominantly Christian and Catholic, residents practiced a diversity of denominations.
